programs.name: Four Pillar Training Program
programs.description: An evidence-based training system built around four pillars — Strength, Endurance, Mobility, and Healthspan. Every exercise and protocol is selected from peer-reviewed research with the goal of keeping you strong, mobile, and capable for life. Not a bodybuilder program. A longevity program.

citations.principle: VO₂ max is the strongest predictor of all-cause mortality — no upper bound of benefit
citations.keyFinding: Low-fit men had 5.04× mortality vs. elite-fit; stronger predictor than smoking or diabetes

citations.principle: Each 1-MET VO₂ max increase = 13–15% lower all-cause mortality
citations.keyFinding: n=750,302 US veterans; linear association, no plateau

citations.principle: Norwegian 4×4 protocol produces 7–13% VO₂ max gain in 8 weeks
citations.keyFinding: 4×4 outperformed all other interval protocols tested

citations.principle: Zone 2 drives mitochondrial biogenesis and fat oxidation via MCT1
citations.keyFinding: Lactate shuttle theory; Zone 2 uniquely targets Type I mitochondria

citations.principle: Lifelong strength training preserves Type II fiber proportion vs. endurance or untrained
citations.keyFinding: Strength-trained masters: 52% Type II fibers; recreationally active: 39.3%

citations.principle: Hypertrophy equal across load spectrum when taken to failure; strength favors heavy load
citations.keyFinding: 21 RCTs; high load ~2× more effective for 1RM strength

citations.principle: Minimum effective dose: 4 sets/muscle/week for growth; 1 set/week for maintenance
citations.keyFinding: Dose-response; diminishing returns pronounced past ~15 sets/week for strength

citations.principle: Each 5 kg lower grip strength = 16% higher all-cause mortality, 17% cardiovascular mortality
citations.keyFinding: n=139,691 across 17 countries; grip stronger predictor than systolic BP

citations.principle: Heavy resistance training (≥85% 1RM) significantly improved BMD; low-intensity did not
citations.keyFinding: 5×5 deadlift + squat + OHP; significant lumbar + femoral neck BMD gains

citations.principle: Nordic curl inclusion halves hamstring injury rates
citations.keyFinding: n=8,459 athletes; 51% reduction in hamstring strain risk

citations.principle: Trap-bar deadlift: ~10% lower spinal moment, higher power vs. conventional
citations.keyFinding: 19 powerlifters; trap bar allows higher loads at lower joint stress

citations.principle: Unilateral training matches bilateral for strength; outperforms for speed + agility
citations.keyFinding: Bulgarian split squat group superior on sprint + change-of-direction tests

citations.principle: Concurrent training causes negligible hypertrophy or strength loss when programmed correctly
citations.keyFinding: 43 studies; interference effect mainly in explosive strength, not hypertrophy

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.notes: Rear foot elevated

exercises.notes: Bird Dog · Side Plank · Modified Curl-Up

exercises.name: Bird Dog
exercises.notes: Opposite arm and leg. Brace before moving. Straight line from heel to fingertips. Hold 2–3 s at top.

exercises.name: Side Plank
exercises.notes: Stack feet or stagger for an easier variation. Keep hips high — don't let them sag.

exercises.name: Modified Curl-Up
exercises.notes: Hands under lumbar spine to maintain natural curve. Lift head and shoulders only — not a full crunch. Brief hold at top.

mobilityRoutines.name: Morning Routine
mobilityRoutines.notes: Do before coffee. Daily consistency beats occasional long sessions.

exercises.name: Hip CARs
exercises.notes: Full hip circle — signals nervous system you own full ROM

exercises.name: Shoulder CARs

benchmarks.name: Weighted Pull-Up

benchmarks.name: VO₂ Max

plans.name: 4-Day Plan

exercises.notes: Heaviest compound of the week. Prioritize form.
Rear foot on bench. At high load rivals barbell squat for quad + glute stimulus.
Hip hinge, not squat. Drive hips through aggressively.
Anchor feet under bench or partner. Slow negative is the stimulus.

exercises.load: Heaviest DBs — grip is the limiter
exercises.notes: Grip, traps, core, posture. Targets Centenarian Decathlon carry benchmark.
Non-negotiable. Hold 2–3 s at top each rep.
Target 20–25 min. Bike or incline walk. Counts toward 150 min/week Zone 2 target.
Best upper-body pull indicator for longevity (grip + back strength). Add weight when you hit 6 reps.
Neutral grip. Safer to push solo than barbell. Full ROM — touch chest.
Chest on incline bench removes lower back from the equation.
Arc-friendly shoulder press. Lower impingement risk than vertical press.
External rotation + horizontal abduction. Essential for climbers.
Decompresses spine, trains grip, builds scap stability. Log your time.
Bike or easy row after lifting.
Counts as upper-body pulling volume for today. Replaces — not supplements — a separate pull session.
Balances the climbing pulling volume.
Essential for climbers. Carry a band in your chalk bag.
Extensor balance for climbing flexors.
5 min bike or row + dynamic stretches
Bike, rower, or incline treadmill. You should not be able to speak during the 4 min intervals. 3 min recovery at easy pace between each.
Lower knee compressive force vs. back squat. Good for long-term knee health.
Hip stability and left-right asymmetry correction.
Anti-lateral-flexion core demand.

plans.goal: The full Four Pillar schedule — lower body, upper body, climbing, and a VO₂ session each week. Strength blocks target 2–3 RIR; Norwegian 4×4 intervals go to near-maximal effort. Built around the four pillars of longevity fitness: strength, VO₂ max, cardiovascular endurance, and movement quality.

plans.name: 3-Day Plan

exercises.notes: 3-day plan uses 3 sets vs 4 to leave energy for the rest of the session.
Hold 2–3 s at top each rep.
Replaces upper-body pull day entirely. If no climbing this week, substitute Wednesday Upper Body from 4-day plan.

workouts.sessionType: Upper Body Push + Pull + VO₂ Max (alternating)

exercises.notes: Do BEFORE lifting on Week A.
Do AFTER lifting on Week B.

plans.goal: A compressed version of the full program — lower and upper strength combined into fuller-body sessions, plus climbing and one VO₂ day. Same intensity targets as the 4-day, less weekly volume. Use this when four sessions aren't realistic.

plans.name: Home Plan
plans.scheduleDescription: Home Day A · Home Day B · Climbing Gym (Sat)  |  Any 3 days that work

exercises.notes: Full hip circle — primes the joint before loading.
Sit in 90/90, lean forward on front shin. Clears internal rotation before loading.
Bridge up, hold, alternately lift knees. Activates glutes before hinge work.
Primary hinge at home. Hip hinge from hips, soft knees, DBs track close to legs. Go as heavy as form allows.
Free-standing, slight knee bend. Trains hip stability and corrects left-right asymmetry.
Adds posterior-chain volume with zero axial load. Good if fatigued from RDLs.
Rear foot on bench. At this load rivals barbell squat for quad + glute stimulus at roughly half the spinal load.
Hold one DB at chest, sit deep. Reinforces squat pattern and adds quad volume.
Drive through heel, stand tall at top. Extra unilateral quad volume.
Grip should be the limiting factor. Targets Centenarian Decathlon carry benchmark.
Top foot on bench, bottom foot off floor. Best adductor + lateral core exercise at home.
Hold 2–3 s at top each rep.
Off step edge. Slow tempo is the point for Achilles and patellar tendon health.
Couch stretch alternative — best hip flexor release after split squats.
90/90 hip switches — converts passive hip mobility to active usable range.
Supine hamstring stretch. Drop if short on time.
Full shoulder circle — critical joint prep before pressing and pulling.
Activates rear delts and scapular retractors before pressing.
Activates serratus anterior and trains upward rotation — counters desk posture.

groups.name: HORIZONTAL PRESS — replaces Neutral-Grip DB Bench + Landmine Press

exercises.notes: Full ROM — touch chest, full lockout. Safer to push hard solo than a barbell: can dump DBs safely.

exercises.load: BW + load via belt or DB between feet
exercises.notes: One of the best pressing movements available at home. Shoulders below elbows at the bottom.
Upper arms contact floor each rep — removes leg drive, isolates chest/triceps.
Seated for ceiling clearance. Overhead strength and shoulder stability. Replaces landmine press.

groups.name: PULL — replaces Weighted Pull-Up + Chest-Supported Row

exercises.notes: Brace opposite hand on bench. Elbow drives back and up. Primary pull at home.
Rings at hip height, body horizontal. Steeper = harder. Best horizontal pull at home. Weather-dependent.
Face-down on incline bench. No lower-back fatigue. Good when rings aren't available.
Anchor at eye height, pull to face with elbows high and wide. Best exercise for rotator cuff + rear delt health.
Elbow tucked, forearm rotates out. Protects external rotators that climbing neglects.
Decompress spine, maintain grip and shoulder health. Progress toward 2-min continuous hang benchmark.
Medial delt — supports overhead stability. Quick to do.
Arm at 90° on doorframe, rotate away. Opens anterior shoulder — essential after pressing.
Walk hands to one side to bias the lat. Climber staple — keeps lats long.
Side-lying, rotate top shoulder toward floor. Decompresses thoracic spine.
Primary reason to be here. Keep progressing — home Bulgarian split squats are maintenance; this is progression.
Home RDLs preserve the pattern; this is where you push weight. Reset fully between reps.
With safeties + rack you can push harder than at home. Your heavy press day.
The one bilateral row where you can load beyond what DBs allow. Mid-back in a way DB rows can't fully replicate.
This IS your Zone 2 + VO₂ stimulus + grip volume + upper pulling for the week. Don't skip climbing for more barbell work.
Heavier than home. Home carries cover maintenance; this pushes the Centenarian Decathlon benchmark.

plans.goal: Full Four Pillar intent adapted for home equipment (Power Blocks, bench, dip bar). Covers lower and upper strength at home plus the climbing day. Use challenging loads — the program assumes access to heavy enough dumbbells. Match gym intensity: 2–3 RIR on strength work.

programs.releaseNotes: Cardio now shows a target zone band on the live bar (Norwegian 4×4 → Z4–Z5). Dedicated Zone 2 blocks hold the countdown until you reach Zone 2 (tap Start Now to skip) so the prescribed time is time *in* zone.

programs.name: General Strength & Conditioning
programs.description: A four-phase progressive program designed to build movement quality, muscle, strength, and cardiovascular fitness across 28+ weeks. Phase 1 (Foundation, 6 wks) establishes movement patterns and connective tissue resilience. Phase 2 (Hypertrophy, 10 wks) accumulates volume for muscle growth. Phase 3 (Strength, 6 wks) converts mass to neural strength. Phase 4 (Conditioning, 6 wks) peaks VO₂ max and cardiovascular fitness. All plans use Dynamic Equipment — at workout start the app resolves each exercise to the best alternative for your available equipment automatically.
programs.targetProfile: General population — beginner to intermediate, home or commercial gym

plans.name: 4-Day | Foundation
plans.goal: Establish movement quality and work capacity before adding serious load. Sets are 10–15 reps at moderate weight — keep 2–3 reps in reserve on everything. Prioritise technique and consistency over pushing numbers. This base makes every phase that follows safer and more effective.

plans.name: 3-Day | Foundation
plans.goal: Establish movement quality and work capacity before adding serious load. Sets are 10–15 reps at moderate weight — keep 2–3 reps in reserve on everything. Prioritise technique and consistency over pushing numbers. This base makes every phase that follows safer and more effective.

plans.name: 4-Day | Hypertrophy

exercises.notes: Mesocycle 1: 3×10–15 / Mesocycle 2: 4×8–12

plans.goal: Accumulate enough volume and mechanical stress to drive muscle growth. 6–15 reps with progressive overload each session — add weight or reps before moving up. Keep 1–2 reps in reserve. Expect soreness early in the block; that's normal. By week 8 you should be hitting new rep-range PRs.

plans.name: 3-Day | Hypertrophy
plans.goal: Accumulate enough volume and mechanical stress to drive muscle growth. 6–15 reps with progressive overload each session — add weight or reps before moving up. Keep 1–2 reps in reserve. Expect soreness early in the block; that's normal. By week 8 you should be hitting new rep-range PRs.

plans.name: 4-Day | Strength

exercises.notes: Heavy compound — prioritize depth and bracing
Heaviest session of the week

plans.goal: Convert hypertrophy gains into peak force output. Lower reps (3–6), heavier loads, close to technical failure on the last set. Rest 2–3 min between sets — that rest is part of the program. Numbers should climb weekly; if they stall two sessions in a row, drop 10 % and rebuild.

plans.name: 3-Day | Strength
plans.goal: Convert hypertrophy gains into peak force output. Lower reps (3–6), heavier loads, close to technical failure on the last set. Rest 2–3 min between sets — that rest is part of the program. Numbers should climb weekly; if they stall two sessions in a row, drop 10 % and rebuild.

plans.name: 4-Day | Conditioning

exercises.notes: Maintenance — moderate load
40–45 min at conversational pace, 65–75% max HR. Prefer cycling or rowing.
4 rounds × 4 min at 90–95% max HR. 3 min active recovery between rounds. Warm up 10 min, cool down 5 min. Prefer bike or rower.

plans.goal: Shift emphasis toward metabolic fitness and cardiovascular capacity alongside strength maintenance. Push hard on conditioning circuits and finishers — these are meant to be uncomfortable. Keep strength sets controlled at 1–2 RIR. The goal is to be strong AND able to sustain effort.

plans.name: 3-Day | Conditioning

exercises.notes: Maintenance lifting — 2–3 sets × 10–12 reps
40–45 min at conversational pace, 65–75% max HR.
4 rounds × 4 min at 90–95% max HR. 3 min active recovery. Warm up 10 min, cool down 5 min.

plans.goal: Shift emphasis toward metabolic fitness and cardiovascular capacity alongside strength maintenance. Push hard on conditioning circuits and finishers — these are meant to be uncomfortable. Keep strength sets controlled at 1–2 RIR. The goal is to be strong AND able to sustain effort.

mobilityRoutines.name: Morning CARs
mobilityRoutines.notes: Do daily — before coffee if possible. CARs (Controlled Articular Rotations) signal the nervous system that you own full joint range. Daily repetition is the mechanism; skipping days reduces the adaptation signal.

exercises.name: Hip CARs
exercises.notes: Standing, one hand on wall for balance. Maximise the circle — reach forward, out, back, and through. Full tension throughout.

exercises.name: Shoulder CARs
exercises.notes: Keep the rest of the body still. Reach long through the full arc. Critical for climbers — maintains capsular health and scapular dissociation.

exercises.name: Thoracic Spine CARs
exercises.notes: Seated or quadruped. Isolate the T-spine — keep the lumbar quiet. Desk work kills thoracic extension; this is the daily antidote.

exercises.name: Wrist CARs
exercises.notes: Both wrists together or one at a time. Maximise the circle in both directions. Underused but essential for climbers — prepares the wrist joint before any gripping or loading.

exercises.name: Chin Tucks
exercises.focusArea: Forward head posture / deep neck flexors
exercises.notes: Activates longus colli — the muscles that atrophy from screen work. Neurological stimulus; no fatigue cost. Draw chin straight back, not down.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Aparts
exercises.notes: Highest-leverage desk-worker and climber corrective. Climbers are chronically dominant in lat/pec/biceps; this rebalances the shoulder girdle daily with near-zero cost. Keep arms straight, squeeze shoulder blades together at end range.

mobilityRoutines.name: Theme A — Thoracic
mobilityRoutines.notes: Desk work drives thoracic kyphosis. Climbing locks the T-spine in flexion under load. Combined, this limits overhead reach, bench press depth, and breathing mechanics. No equipment needed — floor only.

exercises.name: Cat-Cow
exercises.duration: 10 reps slow — full end-range extension and flexion
exercises.notes: Emphasise the full arch (extension) as much as the curl. Most people underdo the extension end.

exercises.name: Thread-the-Needle
exercises.duration: 5 reps/side — 3 sec hold at end range
exercises.notes: From quadruped. Thread one arm under the body and rotate. Use the exhale to sink deeper. Keep hips level.

exercises.name: Quadruped T-Spine Rotation (hand behind head)
exercises.notes: From quadruped, one hand behind head. Rotate elbow up toward ceiling. Keep lower back still — movement comes from mid-back only.

exercises.name: Book Opener (side-lying)
exercises.duration: 5 reps/side — 5 sec hold at end range
exercises.notes: Side-lying, knees stacked at 90°. Sweep top arm across and open chest toward ceiling. Breathe into the stretch. Let gravity do the work.

exercises.name: Prone Cobra Hold
exercises.notes: Face down, arms at sides. Lift chest using mid-back — not lower back. Squeeze shoulder blades down and back. Progress from elbows bent to arms straight.

exercises.name: Seated T-Spine Rotation over Bench Back
exercises.notes: Sit astride a bench facing the back. Rotate and hook elbows on the bench back. Adds a mechanical fulcrum that isolates T-spine better than floor rotations.

mobilityRoutines.name: Theme C — Shoulder & Posterior Capsule
mobilityRoutines.notes: Climbing is relentlessly internally-rotating. High pressing volume compounds this. Tight posterior capsule (GIRD) is the primary driver of shoulder impingement and rotator cuff pathology in climbers. This is the most injury-prevention critical theme. Equipment: resistance band, pull-up bar.

exercises.name: Sleeper Stretch
exercises.notes: Side-lying, bottom arm at 90° shoulder/elbow. Use top hand to gently push forearm toward floor. This is the most direct treatment for GIRD — do not skip. Stop at pain; discomfort is expected.

exercises.name: Cross-Body Shoulder Stretch
exercises.notes: Pull the arm across the chest using the opposite hand at the elbow. Keep shoulder packed down — do not let it shrug up. Complements sleeper stretch with a different capsular angle.

exercises.name: Band External Rotation (elbow at side, 90°)
exercises.focusArea: Rotator cuff ER strength — short range
exercises.notes: Band anchored to side. Elbow pinned to hip. Rotate forearm out slowly (2 sec), resist return (2 sec). This is strengthening, not stretching — control the negative.

exercises.name: Band External Rotation at 90° Abduction
exercises.focusArea: Rotator cuff ER strength — end range (most deficient in climbers)
exercises.notes: Arm out to side at shoulder height. Band from below. Rotate forearm from pointing down to pointing up. This late-range ER position is where climbers have the most deficit and highest injury risk.

exercises.name: Dead Hang
exercises.notes: Full passive hang, allow shoulders to rise toward ears. Then actively depress scapulae (pull shoulders away from ears) — hold 5 sec. Alternate between passive and active. Decompresses the joint and trains scapular retraction under load.

exercises.name: Wall Slide with Posterior Tilt
exercises.focusArea: Lower trap + serratus anterior in overhead position
exercises.notes: Hands on wall at shoulder height. Slide arms up while maintaining light contact. Keep ribs down (posterior pelvic tilt equivalent for the thorax). This trains the muscles that stabilise the scapula overhead — the primary failure point in shoulder impingement.

mobilityRoutines.name: Theme D — Posterior Chain & Hamstrings
mobilityRoutines.notes: Heavy deadlifts and RDLs at 80–90%+ demand full posterior chain length-tension. Desk sitting means hamstrings never get loaded at length. This theme targets the length-tension relationship under load, not just passive flexibility. Equipment: light dumbbell (5–15 lb), bench.

exercises.name: Supine Hamstring Stretch (band or strap)
exercises.notes: Loop a band around the foot. Straighten the leg toward the ceiling. Do not force — let the weight of the leg do the work over 90 seconds. Establish the passive range before loading it.

exercises.name: Active Straight Leg Raise
exercises.focusArea: Nervous system buy-in for hamstring length
exercises.notes: Supine, one leg flat. Kick the other leg up as high as possible using hip flexors — no momentum. Hold 2 sec at top. The contrast between passive range (previous exercise) and active range reveals where the nervous system is restricting you.

exercises.name: Jefferson Curl (5–15 lb DB)
exercises.duration: 5 reps slow — 3 sec down, 1 sec hold, 3 sec up
exercises.focusArea: Full posterior chain under spinal flexion load
exercises.notes: Stand on a step or bench edge. Hold a light DB. Curl down vertebra by vertebra into full spinal flexion, reaching toward the floor. Do not rush. This builds posterior chain strength through full range — including ranges that conventional deadlifts avoid.

exercises.name: Single-Leg RDL with Pause (light DB or bodyweight)
exercises.focusArea: Eccentric hamstring strengthening + hip hinge pattern
exercises.notes: Hinge at hip, send the free leg back. Pause 2 sec at the bottom in maximum hip flexion. Stand by squeezing the glute of the working leg. The pause removes momentum and maximises time under tension at end range.

exercises.name: Nordic Hamstring Curl (feet under bench)
exercises.focusArea: Eccentric hamstring strength — highest evidence for injury prevention
exercises.notes: Kneel, feet anchored under the bench. Lower the body toward the floor as slowly as possible (aim for 5+ sec). Catch yourself with hands. Pull back up using hands as needed. Highest-evidence exercise for hamstring strain prevention. Progress by increasing reps before adding sets.

exercises.name: Hip Hinge with Band (band looped over hips, anchored forward)
exercises.focusArea: Hip hinge motor pattern + posterior loading
exercises.notes: Loop a band over the hip crease, anchored to something in front. Hinge back into the band's resistance. Hold 3 sec. The band provides tactile feedback for where the hinge axis should be and teaches posterior hip loading.

mobilityRoutines.name: Theme E — Ankle Dorsiflexion & Calf
mobilityRoutines.notes: Limited ankle dorsiflexion accounts for up to 43% of variance in squat depth. Zone 2 and VO2 interval sessions create the highest cumulative ankle and calf load of the program — Achilles tendinopathy risk peaks here. Equipment: wall, step edge or bench, resistance band.

exercises.name: Ankle DF Wall Drill
exercises.notes: Toes touching the wall, knee drives toward wall keeping heel flat. Move foot back until the furthest distance where knee can touch. Measure weekly — asymmetry of >2 cm is worth addressing. Keep this as your benchmark.

exercises.name: Half-Kneeling Ankle Mobilization (banded)
exercises.notes: Loop a band around the ankle/heel. Anchor band behind you. Drive the knee forward over the toes — the band provides posterior traction on the talus, creating joint space. This addresses the bony restriction component, not just soft tissue.

exercises.name: Calf Stretch — Bent Knee (soleus bias)
exercises.notes: Against wall, foot flat, knee bent and pushed forward. Soleus is the primary dorsiflexion restrictor in most people (not gastrocnemius). If you feel this more than the straight-knee version, the soleus is your limiting factor.

exercises.name: Calf Stretch — Straight Knee (gastrocnemius bias)
exercises.notes: Against wall or on a step, heel dropped, knee locked. Target the upper calf/back of knee. Do this after the bent-knee version to hit both heads.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Eccentric Calf Raise (off step edge)
exercises.duration: 3 × 15 reps — 3 sec down, 1 sec hold, 3 sec up
exercises.focusArea: Achilles tendon health + strength in dorsiflexion
exercises.notes: Stand on one leg on a step edge. Rise up with both legs if needed; lower on one leg only (3 sec). This is the Alfredson eccentric protocol — the highest-evidence intervention for Achilles tendinopathy prevention and treatment. Also builds calf strength at end-range dorsiflexion.

exercises.name: Toe Spreading + Foot Intrinsic Activation
exercises.notes: Seated. Spread all toes as wide as possible. Then lift just the big toe while keeping the others down; then lift the four small toes while keeping the big toe down. These intrinsic muscles prevent pronation compensation when DF is limited — the chain starts at the foot.

programs.name: Quick Strength
programs.description: Full-body home gym training for limited time. Three interchangeable sessions each cover every major movement pattern in 20–30 minutes via compound supersets. Built on minimum-effective-dose research: 2–3 hard sets per muscle group at RPE 8–9, taken close to failure, is sufficient to maintain and build strength at 1–3 sessions per week (Bickel 2011; Ralston 2017; Schoenfeld 2019). Agonist-antagonist supersets cut session time by ~40% with no loss in performance (Kelleher 2010). Pull:push ratio is 2:1 to support climbing shoulder health.

plans.name: 3-Day Rotation
plans.scheduleDescription: Any 1–3 days/week — each session stands alone
plans.goal: Maintain and build full-body strength with minimal time. Each session hits every major pattern: hinge, squat, push, pull. Lift heavy — compounds should feel challenging by rep 4–5 (RPE 8–9). Two sessions per week produces gradual progress; one session per week preserves what you have. Do the days in any order, skip freely — they're designed to be independent.

exercises.notes: Push hips back, soft knee. Feel the hamstring stretch at the bottom — that's the load, not a lower back pull. Drive hips through to stand tall. Do 1–2 lighter ramp-up sets before your first working set.
2-second controlled descent, explosive press. Full stretch at the bottom. Superset — go directly from the RDL without sitting down.
Elbows track inside knees. Pause 1 second at the bottom. Stay upright.

exercises.load: Heavy — as heavy as the RDL if possible
exercises.notes: Row the elbow to the hip, full range of motion. Brace the core — don't rotate the torso. Complete both sides before resting.

exercises.load: Heavy — grip should be the limiting factor
exercises.notes: Stand tall, core braced. Walk at a controlled pace. Turn around at 20m. Skip if time is short — the supersets above are the priority.
Front foot far enough forward that your shin stays vertical at the bottom. Hold dumbbells at your sides. Complete both legs before the row.
Pull elbow to hip. Keep chest square to the floor — don't twist.
Seated or standing. Neutral grip (palm facing in) to reduce shoulder stress. Full lockout at top, controlled descent.
Arms straight. Squeeze shoulder blades together at end range. Keeps the rotator cuff and rear deltoids healthy for climbing — do not skip this one.

exercises.load: Bodyweight — add dumbbell between feet if too easy
exercises.notes: Keep torso upright to bias triceps. Lower to 90° elbow flexion.
Lower back pressed into the floor throughout — that's the whole point. Extend opposite arm and leg slowly, return, switch sides. Never let the back arch.

exercises.load: Heavy — same target as Day A
exercises.notes: This is your lower body work for the session — push the load. Hip hinge, feel the hamstring stretch.
Short session means every set counts. Push to RPE 8–9. Full range.
Elbows track knees. Pause 1s at the bottom. Add weight vs. Day A if you've done it recently.
No bench setup required. Lower until elbows touch the floor, press to full lockout. Same movement as bench press with a slightly shorter range.

mobilityRoutines.name: Quick Warm-Up
mobilityRoutines.notes: Do before your first working set. Light, controlled movements only — you're preparing joints, not tiring muscles.

exercises.name: Bodyweight Hip Hinge
exercises.notes: Push hips back, arms reach forward for balance. Feel the hamstring stretch each rep.

exercises.name: World's Greatest Stretch
exercises.notes: Lunge forward, plant hand inside foot, rotate thoracic spine toward ceiling.

exercises.name: Arm Circles + Shoulder Rolls
exercises.notes: Large, controlled circles. Finish with 5 shoulder rolls forward and back.
